sections.heading: File Storage in Android
sections.content: Android provides multiple mechanisms for storing files depending on their size, sensitivity, and whether they should be shared with other apps. Unlike SharedPreferences or Room (which handle structured data), file storage handles raw binary or text data — images, audio, video, PDFs, logs, downloaded content, and user-created documents. Android organizes storage into two primary areas: internal storage (private to your app, always available) and external storage (potentially shared, may require permissions). Since Android 10 (API 29), Google introduced Scoped Storage, fundamentally changing how apps access files on external storage.

sections.heading: Storage Options Overview
items: App-specific internal storage — private directory on internal memory; no permissions needed; deleted when app is uninstalled
App-specific external storage — private directory on external storage (SD card or emulated); no permissions needed on API 29+
Shared storage (MediaStore) — photos, videos, audio, and downloads shared across apps; requires permissions on older APIs
Documents and files (SAF) — Storage Access Framework lets users pick files from any provider using a system file picker
Assets (read-only) — files bundled in the app's assets/ folder; read-only at runtime
Raw resources — files in res/raw/ accessed via R.raw.filename; read-only at runtime
Cache directories — temporary files that the system can delete when storage is low
DataStore and Room — for structured data and preferences, not raw file storage

rows.best_for: Sensitive app data, config files, private user data
Temporary files, downloaded thumbnails, decompressed assets

rows.permissions: None on API 29+; READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE on older
rows.best_for: Large files the user may need after uninstall (backup photos, exports)
Large temporary files like downloaded media for offline playback

rows.permissions: READ_MEDIA_IMAGES / READ_MEDIA_VIDEO on API 33+; READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE on older
rows.accessible_by_others: Yes — visible to all apps and gallery
rows.best_for: Photos and videos the user creates and wants to keep in the gallery

rows.permissions: None — user grants access via picker
rows.accessible_by_others: Yes — user explicitly selects the file
rows.persists_after_uninstall: Yes (file is not owned by your app)
rows.best_for: Opening, editing, or saving documents picked by the user

sections.heading: Internal Storage
sections.content: Internal storage is the safest and simplest place to store files that are private to your app. Every app has its own internal storage directory that no other app can access (unless the device is rooted). The primary directories are context.filesDir for persistent files and context.cacheDir for temporary files. Files in filesDir persist until the user uninstalls the app or clears app data. Files in cacheDir may be deleted by the system when storage is critically low. No permissions are needed to read or write to either directory.

sections.heading: Reading and Writing Internal Files
items: Get the files directory: val file = File(context.filesDir, "my_file.txt")
Write text to a file: file.writeText("Hello World") — creates or overwrites the file
Append text: file.appendText("More content")
Write bytes: file.writeBytes(byteArray)
Read text: val content = file.readText()
Read lines: val lines = file.readLines()
Read bytes: val bytes = file.readBytes()
Check if file exists: if (file.exists()) { ... }
Delete a file: file.delete()
Create subdirectory: File(context.filesDir, "subfolder").mkdirs()
Use openFileOutput() for stream-based writing: context.openFileOutput("file.txt", Context.MODE_PRIVATE).use { it.write(data) }
Use openFileInput() for stream-based reading: context.openFileInput("file.txt").use { it.readBytes() }

rows.behavior: Creates or overwrites the file — default and recommended mode
Opens the file and appends new content to the end without truncating
Made file readable by other apps — deprecated in API 17; use FileProvider instead
Made file writable by other apps — deprecated in API 17; use FileProvider instead

sections.heading: External App-Specific Storage
sections.content: External app-specific storage gives your app a private directory on external storage (which may be an SD card or emulated external storage on internal memory). Since Android 10 (API 29), this directory is scoped and does not require any storage permissions to access. The path is returned by context.getExternalFilesDir(type) where type is an Environment constant like Environment.DIRECTORY_PICTURES, DIRECTORY_DOCUMENTS, or null for the root. Files here survive app clear data but are deleted on uninstall.

sections.heading: External App-Specific Storage Usage
items: Get pictures directory: val dir = context.getExternalFilesDir(Environment.DIRECTORY_PICTURES)
Get documents directory: context.getExternalFilesDir(Environment.DIRECTORY_DOCUMENTS)
Get music directory: context.getExternalFilesDir(Environment.DIRECTORY_MUSIC)
Get root external directory: context.getExternalFilesDir(null)
Always null-check — external storage may not be available: if (dir != null) { ... }
Check availability: Environment.getExternalStorageState() == Environment.MEDIA_MOUNTED
No permissions needed on API 29 and above for app-specific external directories
Files are accessible via a file manager app on the device but not shared with the media store
Use this for large files (exported CSVs, recorded audio) the user may want to access outside the app

rows.use_for: Image files captured or saved by the app
PDFs, text files, exports, and user documents
Downloaded files the user may want to access later
Audio files generated or cached by the app
Video files recorded or downloaded by the app
Camera-captured photos and videos (standard camera directory)
Custom subdirectories not covered by standard constants

sections.heading: Scoped Storage & MediaStore
sections.content: Since Android 10 (API 29), Scoped Storage restricts apps from freely accessing the entire external storage file system. Apps can only access their own app-specific directories without permissions. To save or read media files (photos, videos, audio) that should appear in the device gallery or be shared with other apps, you must use the MediaStore API. MediaStore is a system content provider that manages shared media collections and provides structured access through URIs.

sections.heading: Saving an Image to MediaStore (Gallery)
items: Create ContentValues describing the file: contentValues.put(MediaStore.Images.Media.DISPLAY_NAME, "photo.jpg")
Set MIME type: contentValues.put(MediaStore.Images.Media.MIME_TYPE, "image/jpeg")
Set relative path (API 29+): contentValues.put(MediaStore.Images.Media.RELATIVE_PATH, "Pictures/MyApp")
Insert into MediaStore: val uri = contentResolver.insert(MediaStore.Images.Media.EXTERNAL_CONTENT_URI, contentValues)
Write file content via the URI: contentResolver.openOutputStream(uri!!)?.use { outputStream -> bitmap.compress(Bitmap.CompressFormat.JPEG, 95, outputStream) }
The image now appears in the device gallery — visible to all apps and the user
On API 29+: set IS_PENDING = 1 before writing and IS_PENDING = 0 after to prevent partial files from appearing in gallery
Read permissions required for reading other apps' media: READ_MEDIA_IMAGES (API 33+) or READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE (pre-API 33)

rows.permission_to_read: No special permission needed for own downloads

sections.heading: Storage Access Framework (SAF)
sections.content: The Storage Access Framework (SAF) allows users to grant your app access to specific files or directories through a system-provided file picker — without requiring broad storage permissions. The user selects a file or folder from any installed document provider (Google Drive, Dropbox, local storage), and the system returns a URI your app can read from or write to. SAF is the recommended approach for apps that need to open, edit, or save user-chosen documents.

sections.heading: SAF — Opening and Creating Files
items: Open a file for reading: use GetContent() contract — launcher.launch("*/*") to let user pick any file
Open an image specifically: launcher.launch("image/*") restricts the picker to image files
Create a new file: use CreateDocument() contract — launcher.launch("text/plain") opens save dialog
Open a directory tree: use OpenDocumentTree() contract — user grants persistent access to a folder
Read the picked file: contentResolver.openInputStream(uri)?.use { stream -> stream.readBytes() }
Write to the picked file: contentResolver.openOutputStream(uri)?.use { stream -> stream.write(data) }
Take persistent URI permission: contentResolver.takePersistableUriPermission(uri, Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION)
Store the URI string and reuse it across app sessions — persistent permissions survive app restarts

rows.use_case: Let user pick an existing file from any source

rows.output: URI? of selected file with persistent permission
rows.use_case: Open a specific document for editing with persistent access
Let user pick multiple files at once
Create and save a new file in a location the user chooses
Grant persistent read/write access to an entire folder

rows.output: URI? of selected photo or video
rows.use_case: Modern photo/video picker using the Android Photo Picker (API 33+)

sections.heading: FileProvider — Sharing Files Between Apps
items: FileProvider is a ContentProvider subclass that exposes private internal files to other apps via a content:// URI
Required for sharing files (e.g., sharing an image to WhatsApp) without granting world-readable permissions

Declare in AndroidManifest.xml inside <application>: <provider android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider" android:authorities="${applicationId}.provider" android:exported="false" android:grantUriPermissions="true" />
Create res/xml/file_paths.xml to define which directories the FileProvider can expose
Get a shareable URI: val uri = FileProvider.getUriForFile(context, "${packageName}.provider", file)
Grant the receiving app permission: intent.addFlags(Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION)
Share via Intent: intent.setDataAndType(uri, "image/jpeg"); startActivity(Intent.createChooser(intent, "Share"))
Never share file:// URIs directly on API 24+ — it throws FileUriExposedException

sections.heading: Capturing Photos with the Camera
items: Use TakePicture() ActivityResultContract for capturing a photo with the system camera app
Create a temporary file in cacheDir or external files dir to store the captured image
Get a FileProvider URI for the file: val photoUri = FileProvider.getUriForFile(context, authority, photoFile)
Launch the camera: takePictureLauncher.launch(photoUri)
The result callback receives a Boolean — true means the photo was saved successfully to the URI
Load the captured image: binding.imageView.setImageURI(photoUri) or decode with BitmapFactory
For video capture, use TakeVideo() contract which returns a Bitmap thumbnail; for full video, use CameraX
REQUEST_IMAGE_CAPTURE via Intent is the legacy approach — prefer ActivityResultContracts for modern code

rows.notes: Broad file system access; permissions declared in manifest and requested at runtime

rows.writing_external: No permission needed for own MediaStore contributions
rows.notes: Scoped Storage introduced; app-specific dirs need no permissions

rows.writing_external: MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE for broad access (requires Play Store approval)
rows.notes: Scoped Storage fully enforced; MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE for file manager apps only

rows.writing_external: No permission for app's own MediaStore contributions
rows.notes: READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE replaced by granular media permissions; Photo Picker available

sections.heading: Best Practices for File Storage
items: Always perform file I/O on a background thread — use coroutines with Dispatchers.IO to avoid blocking the main thread
Use context.cacheDir for temporary files — the system cleans these up when storage is low
Delete temporary files explicitly after use — do not rely solely on the system cache cleanup
Use FileProvider instead of file:// URIs when sharing files with other apps
Prefer the Photo Picker (PickVisualMedia) over requesting READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE for selecting media
Use the Storage Access Framework for opening user documents — avoids broad storage permissions
Always close streams in a finally block or use Kotlin's use { } extension to prevent resource leaks
Compress images before saving — use Bitmap.compress() with an appropriate quality setting to reduce file size

sections.heading: Common File Storage Mistakes to Avoid

rows.mistake: Performing file I/O on the main thread
rows.fix: Use withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { } in a coroutine for all file read and write operations

rows.mistake: Sharing files with file:// URI on API 24+
rows.fix: Use FileProvider.getUriForFile() to create a content:// URI and grant read permission via FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION
No write permission needed for app-specific external directories or MediaStore contributions on API 29+
Use Kotlin's use { } extension on InputStream and OutputStream — it closes the stream automatically

rows.mistake: Storing sensitive data in external storage
rows.fix: Keep sensitive files in internal storage (filesDir) — external storage is accessible to other apps on older APIs
Always build paths using context.filesDir, context.cacheDir, or Environment constants — never hardcode /sdcard/

rows.mistake: Loading large images directly into memory
rows.fix: Decode with BitmapFactory.Options inSampleSize to downsample large images; use Glide or Coil for image loading

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android's file storage system is layered and permission-aware. Use internal storage (filesDir, cacheDir) for private app data with no permissions needed. Use external app-specific directories for larger files the user might access outside the app. Use MediaStore for media files that should appear in the gallery and be shared with other apps. Use the Storage Access Framework for user-driven document access without broad permissions. Always perform I/O on background threads with coroutines, use FileProvider for sharing, and prefer the modern Photo Picker and SAF APIs to avoid requesting broad storage permissions that increasingly restrictive Android versions no longer allow.
